skill.name: mcp-tool-creation
skill.description: Guide developers in creating Rust MCP tools with rmcp macros, typed parameters, schemas, errors, and tests.
skill.summary: Build type-safe Rust MCP tools with rmcp patterns and review guidance.
skill.author: EmilLindfors

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many command execution, weak crypto, network, and environment access patterns. Review found the command execution and weak crypto findings are false positives from markdown and Rust examples, with no prompt injection or runnable malicious code. Medium risk remains because several examples could teach unsafe API key handling, arbitrary SQL execution, and unrestricted file reads.

medium_findings.title: API Key Included in Query URL Example
medium_findings.description: The external API example stores an API key and interpolates it into a request URL. This is documentation code rather than an active payload, but it can teach a pattern that leaks secrets through logs, proxies, and browser history.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The example clearly places self.api_key into a URL string. The risk is instructional because the skill is documentation only, not executable code.

medium_findings.title: Arbitrary SQL Execution Example
medium_findings.description: The database example accepts a SQL string parameter and passes it directly to sqlx::query. This can encourage MCP tools that execute untrusted model or user input as database commands.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The tool parameter is named sql and is passed directly to the query API. The risk is high in copied implementations, but the skill itself only documents the pattern.

medium_findings.title: Unrestricted File Read Example
medium_findings.description: The file read examples accept a path string and read it without demonstrating allowlists, workspace boundaries, or permission checks. This could encourage tools that expose arbitrary local files.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples directly read a caller-provided path. Legitimate file tools can use this pattern, but safe boundary checks are not shown.

low_findings.title: Static Command Execution Findings Dismissed
low_findings.description: The repeated external command detections are false positives caused by markdown code fences, inline backticks, Rust attributes, and Rust method examples. No Ruby shell backtick execution or shell command dispatcher was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review showed the flagged content is Rust documentation and markdown formatting. No executable shell syntax is present in the skill package.

low_findings.title: Static Weak Crypto and Reconnaissance Findings Dismissed
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography and reconnaissance alerts are false positives from generic tokens in prose and sample Rust code. No cryptographic implementation, system discovery routine, or malicious reconnaissance intent was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file is a guide for MCP tool design. Review found no hashing, encryption, credential theft, host discovery, or prompt injection behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: Secret in URL Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: The guide demonstrates placing an API key in a formatted URL. Safer examples should use headers or provider-specific authenticated clients and avoid logging full request URLs.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The exact URL formatting pattern includes key={}. This is a clear unsafe pattern when copied into production code.

dangerous_patterns.title: Raw SQL Tool Parameter Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: The guide demonstrates a tool that accepts raw SQL and executes it. Safer examples should use parameterized queries, query builders, and explicit operation allowlists.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The sample passes a caller-controlled SQL string directly into sqlx::query. The unsafe pattern is explicit in the documentation.

content.value_statement: Developers need clear patterns for creating MCP tools that assistants can call reliably. This skill explains rmcp tool macros, typed parameters, schemas, errors, testing, and practical design patterns.
actual_capabilities: Explains the rmcp #[tool] macro and tool_box pattern.
Shows simple, optional, complex, array, and enum parameter patterns.
Describes return types, structured responses, and Result-based errors.
Provides CRUD, validation, external API, session, and database examples.
Covers unit tests, property tests, and async performance guidance.
Guides review of tool descriptions, type safety, and error handling.
limitations: It is a documentation guide, not a runnable MCP server template.
Some examples need stronger production security controls before reuse.
It focuses on Rust rmcp and does not cover other MCP SDKs deeply.
It does not provide complete dependency manifests or deployment steps.

use_cases.title: Design a New MCP Tool
use_cases.description: Plan parameters, return types, descriptions, and validation for a new Rust MCP tool.

use_cases.title: Review Tool API Shape
use_cases.description: Check whether an MCP tool uses clear names, strong types, useful errors, and safe async behavior.

use_cases.title: Teach MCP Tool Patterns
use_cases.description: Use compact examples to explain macros, schema generation, and tests to a team adopting MCP.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Tool
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner task for a single simple tool.
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me create a Rust rmcp tool for [task]. Define the parameters, return type, description, and basic validation.

prompt_templates.title: Model Complex Parameters
prompt_templates.prompt: Design an rmcp tool that accepts a structured request for [workflow]. Include serde and JsonSchema fields, defaults, and validation rules.

prompt_templates.title: Add Error Handling and Tests
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this MCP tool design for error handling and tests. Suggest Result types, clear error messages, unit tests, and edge cases.

prompt_templates.title: Audit a Tool Suite
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit these Rust MCP tools for API design, type safety, async behavior, permissions, secret handling, and test coverage.

output_examples.input: Request: design a tool that searches documents with optional filters.
output_examples.output: A structured tool plan with a search request object, optional limit and offset fields, validation notes, and expected results.

output_examples.input: Request: review a tool that calls an external service.
output_examples.output: A review covering parameter types, secret handling, timeout behavior, error mapping, and tests for failed service responses.

output_examples.input: Request: improve tests for an arithmetic MCP tool.
output_examples.output: A concise test plan with happy path tests, invalid input checks, boundary cases, and property-based test ideas.

best_practices: Use strong parameter types and derive schemas for structured inputs.
Validate inputs before side effects and return clear Result errors.
Add tests for success paths, invalid inputs, and async failure modes.
anti_patterns: Do not expose raw SQL or filesystem paths without strict permissions.
Do not place API keys in URLs or tool responses.
Do not use vague tool descriptions that hide side effects.

faq.question: What does this skill help me build?
faq.answer: It helps you design and review Rust MCP tools using the rmcp crate and #[tool] macros.

faq.question: Does it generate a full MCP server?
faq.answer: No. It explains tool-level patterns and examples, but it is not a complete server scaffold.

faq.question: Can I use it with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The marketplace metadata lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as supported tools.

faq.question: Is the guidance specific to Rust?
faq.answer: Yes. The examples focus on Rust, rmcp, serde, schemars, tokio, and related crates.

faq.question: Are the examples production ready?
faq.answer: Some examples are illustrative. Add permission checks, secret protection, validation, and tests before production use.

faq.question: What security review is recommended?
faq.answer: Review any tool that reads files, calls networks, stores secrets, or executes database operations.
